The Space Cowboys are betting on him developing into a genuine frontline ace.
695505.recentAnalysis: Burns has made an impressive early statement for Sugar Land: 4 GS, 22.1 IP, 2.42 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 22 K — the raw stuff is translating into results with solid strikeout numbers and excellent run prevention.

695506.name: Jac Caglianone
695506.background: One of the Manhattan Mega Rats' most intriguing young position player investments, Jac Caglianone arrived as a college two-way player whose bat-first skills — enormous raw power, strong athleticism, and advanced feel for hitting — convinced the Mega Rats to prioritize him as a position player keeper. His enormous frame and elite bat speed project toward plus power at maturity, and Manhattan is betting he develops into one of the CBA's more dangerous left-handed bats. He's a long-term investment with a genuinely loud ceiling.
695506.recentAnalysis: Caglianone has had a modest debut for the Mega Rats: .270/.321/.405 with 0 HR and just 1 RBI through 19 games — the power the Mega Rats are counting on hasn't shown up yet, though contact skills look solid.

695578.name: James Wood
695578.background: James Wood is one of the most physically imposing hitters in baseball, a 6-foot-6 outfielder for the Nationals who combines an enormous wingspan with elite bat speed and surprising plate discipline for his age. The Sugar Land Space Cowboys landed him as a cornerstone young bat alongside Lindor and Rice, betting on his massive ceiling as a potential multi-category superstar in the making. Wood's combination of power, speed, and contact upside gives the Space Cowboys one of their most exciting long-term assets.
695578.recentAnalysis: Wood is showing his potential with 7 HR, 18 RBI, and a .931 OPS through 22 games — a powerful early statement that has the Space Cowboys very excited about their big outfielder.

695657.name: Colson Montgomery
695657.background: One of the top free agent shortstops in the CBA, Colson Montgomery is a highly-touted prospect from the White Sox system with a patient approach, developing power, and the athleticism to play premium defense at the position. Available to any CBA team willing to make the investment, he profiles as a .270-hitting shortstop with 20-HR upside once he reaches his ceiling. His elite walk rates and improving contact make him a compelling pickup for any roster in need of shortstop help.
695657.recentAnalysis: Montgomery has had a tough early stretch: .192/.271/.356 (0.717 OPS) with 4 HR and 12 RBI through 22 games — the power is present but the batting average and strikeout rate (27 K in 85 PA) are areas of concern.

695734.name: Daylen Lile
695734.background: Daylen Lile is a toolsy young outfielder for the Nationals who rose quickly through the system behind strong contact skills and promising athleticism, earning his way onto Washington's big-league roster as an everyday contributor. The Portland Chinook grabbed him as one of their exciting youth investments, pairing him with Trea Turner and Cole Ragans for a well-rounded roster. Lile's ability to make contact and put balls in play makes him a reliable counting-stats producer for the Chinook.
695734.recentAnalysis: Lile is hitting .283 with 26 hits and 8 RBI through 21 games for Washington — a solid contact-based start that bodes well for the Chinook's outfield.

696100.name: Hunter Goodman
696100.background: A power-hitting catcher on the Ft. Meade Folksy Ferrets roster, Hunter Goodman emerged from the Rockies' system with one of the better raw power tools among catching prospects, generating impressive exit velocities and home run distances in his early MLB opportunities. The Ferrets secured him as a developmental catcher keep with the upside of a 25-HR backstop if his contact skills develop. At Coors Field he has one of the best home run-friendly environments in baseball to help him post counting stats.
696100.recentAnalysis: Goodman has shown the power the Ferrets are counting on: .257/.321/.527 with 5 HR and 8 RBI through 20 games — though 31 strikeouts in 78 PA is an extremely elevated rate that will determine his ultimate ceiling.

696149.name: Bubba Chandler
696149.background: One of the Manhattan Mega Rats' most intriguing young rotation pieces, Bubba Chandler is a two-way prospect who Pittsburgh developed as a starting pitcher after he showed premium arm talent that was too good to pass up. His mid-90s fastball and advanced secondary stuff give him genuine ace upside, and the Mega Rats grabbed him as a keeper betting on his development trajectory continuing upward. If his command refines, he could become one of the better young starters in CBA.
696149.recentAnalysis: Chandler has made a solid start for the Mega Rats: 4 GS, 20.0 IP, 3.15 ERA, 1.30 WHIP, 17 K — the ERA is excellent even if the WHIP suggests some traffic on the basepaths in his early outings.

696285.name: Jacob Young
696285.background: A free agent outfielder in CBA with solid speed, Jacob Young has shown the athleticism and contact skills to contribute as an everyday outfielder for Washington — bringing stolen base ability and decent batting average from the outfield position. Available for any CBA team looking for speed-focused outfield depth, he profiles as a .265-hitting OF with double-digit stolen base upside when given consistent playing time.
696285.recentAnalysis: Young has been decent in his opportunities: .254/.277/.343 with 2 HR and 8 RBI across 20 games — solid contact and modest counting stats from a player who could contribute more if given a regular starting role.

700241.name: Michael McGreevy
700241.background: A free agent starting pitcher in CBA with solid command and developing stuff, Michael McGreevy has shown an advanced feel for pitching in his Cardinals appearances — featuring excellent control and a pitch mix that generates weak contact and keeps walks to a minimum. Available for any CBA team looking for rotation depth, he profiles as a command-first mid-rotation piece whose ERA tends to exceed his stuff profile through efficient pitching.
700241.recentAnalysis: McGreevy has been solid in his early 2026 starts: 4 GS, 21.2 IP, 2.49 ERA, 0.83 WHIP, 12 K — excellent ERA and WHIP with modest strikeouts, making him an intriguing pickup for teams needing ratio-friendly rotation depth.

700250.name: Ben Rice
700250.background: Ben Rice is a young first baseman/outfielder for the Yankees who burst onto the scene with an unexpected power surge in his first big-league exposure, showcasing premium left-handed pop that immediately drew comparisons to elite power profiles. The Sugar Land Space Cowboys kept him as one of their most exciting young bats alongside Lindor and Crow-Armstrong, banking on his raw power translating into consistent 30-HR production. Rice's ability to hit for average while slugging gives him a ceiling the Space Cowboys are eager to see realized.
700250.recentAnalysis: Rice is putting on a show early in 2026 with a 1.306 OPS, 8 HR, and 18 RBI through just 21 games — the Space Cowboys are getting a steal at this position.

700932.name: Kyle Manzardo
700932.background: A free agent first baseman in CBA with developing power, Kyle Manzardo is a young left-handed hitter who has shown solid plate discipline and the raw power to project toward 20-plus homers at maturity. Available for any CBA team looking for first base depth, he profiles as a patient .265-hitting first baseman with improving power as he gains MLB experience. His walk rates and developing bat make him worth monitoring.
700932.recentAnalysis: Manzardo has had a slow start: .180/.243/.262 with 1 HR and 3 RBI through 19 games — 25 strikeouts in 68 PA is concerning, though his walk rate shows the discipline is still present.

701350.name: Roman Anthony
701350.background: Roman Anthony is one of the most hyped prospects in Red Sox history, a left-handed hitting outfielder with a polished approach and plus raw power who reached Boston ahead of schedule and immediately flashed his potential. The Bristol Banshees secured him as a developmental keeper, betting that Anthony's advanced hit tool translates quickly at the big-league level alongside their other blue-chip assets. His ceiling as a middle-of-the-order hitter is among the highest in the entire CBA.
701350.recentAnalysis: Anthony is showing patience at the plate through 20 games for Boston with a .236 AVG — the Banshees are content letting him develop, knowing the power and average numbers should follow.

701358.name: Cam Smith
701358.background: One of the most exciting young hitters on the Syracuse Sky Chiefs roster, Cam Smith arrived as one of the top position player prospects in baseball with a polished approach and plus bat speed that generated immediate comparisons to elite contact hitters. His ability to work counts, hit for average, and project toward 20-plus homer power makes him a potential franchise cornerstone for Syracuse. The Sky Chiefs locked him up as a keeper anticipating that this is only the beginning of a long career as a CBA impact bat.
701358.recentAnalysis: Smith has had a promising start for Syracuse: .257/.352/.449 (0.802 OPS) with 3 HR, 10 RBI, 13 runs, and 3 SB across 22 games — solid early contributions across multiple categories from a player who is still just scratching his ceiling.

701538.name: Cole Young
701538.background: A highly anticipated shortstop prospect for Seattle, Cole Young is a switch-hitter who features a polished advanced approach at the plate — exceptional contact rates and on-base skills for his age, with developing power and speed. He was one of the more buzzed-about 2025 call-ups and figures to be a centerpiece of the Mariners for years to come.
701538.recentAnalysis: Young is having an outstanding early MLB career with .267/.386/.391 and 3 HR and 1 SB through 114 PA — the contact and walks are exactly what scouts projected and the power is developing nicely.

701542.name: Will Warren
701542.background: A free agent starting pitcher in CBA with excellent early results, Will Warren has quickly established himself as a productive starter with a diverse pitch mix, solid command, and the ability to generate strikeouts throughout his outings. Available for any CBA team looking to add rotation depth, he's one of the better free agent starting pitcher values available — and his EROSP ranking understates his current impact given his hot start.
701542.recentAnalysis: Warren has been excellent in 2026: 5 GS, 25.1 IP, 2.49 ERA, 1.11 WHIP, 31 K — an outstanding early stretch that makes him one of the most appealing pitching free agents available in CBA right now.

701762.name: Nick Kurtz
701762.background: Nick Kurtz burst onto the scene as one of the most celebrated first base prospects in years, a left-handed slugger out of Wake Forest who rocketed through the Athletics system on the strength of advanced plate discipline and big raw power. The Ghent Whistlepigs grabbed him as one of their most prized keepers, betting on his upside as Oakland's everyday first baseman for years to come. His blend of contact, walks, and pop gives him a high floor and a ceiling that has the Whistlepigs excited.
701762.recentAnalysis: Kurtz is hitting .235 with 2 HR through 21 games for Oakland — still finding his big-league footing, but the Whistlepigs have all the patience in the world for a player with this profile.

702070.name: Noah Cameron
702070.background: A free agent starting pitcher in CBA with developing stuff, Noah Cameron has worked his way into a rotation role with solid secondary pitches and improving command. Available for any CBA team looking for rotation depth, he profiles as a development-track starter who could develop into a mid-rotation piece if his command continues to sharpen.
702070.recentAnalysis: Cameron has had mixed early results: 4 GS, 20.0 IP, 5.40 ERA, 1.45 WHIP, 17 K — the strikeout rate shows the stuff is real but command issues have elevated his ERA in the early going.

702332.name: Caleb Durbin
702332.background: Caleb Durbin is a speedy utility man for the Brewers who has made himself valuable with his plus speed and ability to play multiple positions, offering stolen base upside in a contact-oriented package. The Portland Chinook grabbed him as a speed play alongside Trea Turner and Buxton, betting that his legs and versatility will add a stolen base dimension to their already talented lineup. Durbin's athleticism and ability to plug holes around the diamond give the Chinook roster flexibility.
702332.recentAnalysis: Durbin is struggling with a .133 AVG and .418 OPS through 19 games — a very rough stretch for the Chinook, who are hoping for a turnaround from their speed-first utility man.

702616.name: Jackson Holliday
702616.background: One of the Portland Chinook's most exciting young investments, Jackson Holliday arrived as arguably the top prospect in all of baseball — a generational shortstop talent whose combination of elite plate discipline, advanced bat-to-ball skills, and projectable power has drawn comparisons to some of the game's all-time greats. Portland locked him up as a long-term keeper knowing his ceiling is that of a perennial MVP candidate, and the Chinook have positioned themselves perfectly to benefit from his ascent. He is the crown jewel of Portland's rebuild.
702616.recentAnalysis: Holliday is currently on the 10-day IL and has not yet recorded 2026 stats — the Chinook are eagerly awaiting the return of their most prized asset.

800048.name: Parker Messick
800048.background: The breakout star of the Sugar Land Space Cowboys pitching staff, Parker Messick arrived as a crafty left-handed starter with a deceptive delivery, excellent command, and the ability to generate weak contact through pitch tunneling and sequencing. His development through the system was noted for his advanced feel for pitching beyond his years, and the Space Cowboys locked him up as a keeper anticipating exactly this kind of dominant debut. He's quickly become one of the most exciting young arms in all of CBA.
800048.recentAnalysis: Messick has been absolutely dominant for Sugar Land to open 2026: 4 GS, 25.2 IP, 1.05 ERA, 0.78 WHIP, 25 K — one of the best early-season pitching lines in the league and an immediate argument for ace consideration.

801403.name: Chase Dollander
801403.background: A free agent starting pitcher in CBA with significant upside, Chase Dollander was one of the most heralded college pitching prospects in recent years — featuring a premium fastball and developing secondary arsenal that generated enormous buzz before he was drafted by Colorado. Available for any CBA team to claim, he profiles as a development-track starter with top-of-rotation ceiling if his command develops at the MLB level. His youth and stuff make him one of the more compelling free agent pitching investments.
801403.recentAnalysis: Dollander has been effective in a bullpen role: 5 appearances, 19.0 IP, 3.32 ERA, 1.11 WHIP, 23 K — excellent strikeout rate contributing nicely in his early MLB opportunities.

802415.name: Chandler Simpson
802415.background: Chandler Simpson is one of the fastest players in baseball, a lightning-quick utility man for the Rays whose elite sprint speed and ability to reach base through contact and walks make him one of the most dangerous baserunners in the game. The Syracuse Sky Chiefs kept him as their speed specialist, knowing that even moderate playing time translates to significant stolen base production that boosts their weekly totals. Simpson's legs give the Sky Chiefs a stolen base floor that few other players can provide.
802415.recentAnalysis: Simpson is a fantasy gold mine through 21 games — .333 AVG, 7 SB, and 28 hits — the Sky Chiefs are getting elite stolen base production from their speedster.

805249.name: Otto Kemp
805249.background: A free agent infielder in CBA in the very early stages of his MLB career, Otto Kemp is developing his tools at the highest level and working toward a more regular role. Available as a speculative depth add for CBA teams, his early sample is too small to evaluate meaningfully but the underlying tools have drawn interest.
805249.recentAnalysis: Kemp has had just 22 PA across 10 games in 2026 (.100 AVG, 0 HR) — far too limited a sample to draw conclusions about his current level.

805300.name: Jakob Marsee
805300.background: Jakob Marsee is a lightning-quick outfielder for the Marlins who made his big-league debut as one of the most prolific base stealers in the minor leagues, a left-handed hitter with serious wheels and the contact skills to get on base. The North Georgia Fuzzy Bottoms grabbed him as an upside speed play, betting that his stolen base ability translates quickly and adds a category dimension the rest of their roster lacks. His legs make him relevant in a CBA scoring system that rewards steals.
805300.recentAnalysis: Marsee has 8 stolen bases through 22 games but is hitting just .155 — the speed is real and producing for the Fuzzy Bottoms, but the average needs serious work.

805367.name: Chase Meidroth
805367.background: A free agent utility infielder in CBA with outstanding plate discipline, Chase Meidroth arrived with some of the best walk rates among recent prospect hitters — featuring a truly elite eye at the plate that could produce extraordinary OBP if his contact skills develop. Available for any CBA team looking for patient depth, he profiles as a .265-hitting utility player with elite walk production and developing power as he continues to mature.
805367.recentAnalysis: Meidroth has made an encouraging start: .260/.363/.342 with 1 HR, 2 RBI, and 9 walks through 20 games — the walk rate is exactly what scouts projected and he's generating on-base value even without loud counting stats.

805779.name: Jacob Wilson
805779.background: One of the top CBA free agents and a highly-touted young shortstop with one of the best pure contact profiles among prospects in recent memory, Jacob Wilson has generated enormous buzz with elite bat-to-ball skills and a disciplined approach that projects toward .300-plus averages at maturity. Available for any CBA team to claim, he represents a significant upgrade over most rostered middle infielders and his contact ceiling is generational. Any manager who adds him is getting a potential batting title contender.
805779.recentAnalysis: Wilson has had a modest early showing: .253/.264/.368 with 2 HR, 10 RBI, and only 1 walk in 88 PA — the contact is there but the power and approach are still developing in this early MLB sample.

806960.name: Luis Morales
806960.background: A free agent pitcher in CBA currently on non-active roster status (RM), Luis Morales is a young arm with upside working through the development process. Available as a speculative add for teams willing to monitor his progression.
806960.recentAnalysis: Morales has made 2 starts with very difficult results (7.1 IP, 12.27 ERA) and is primarily on non-active roster status — not currently contributing to CBA rosters.

807712.name: Luke Keaschall
807712.background: Luke Keaschall is one of baseball's most exciting young prospects, a speedy contact hitter for the Twins who made a rapid ascent through the minors on the strength of his on-base skills and stolen base ability. The Manhattan Mega Rats secured him as a high-upside keeper, banking on his athleticism and contact-first approach to develop into a multi-category fantasy asset alongside Acuna and Caminero. At just 22, he represents the future of the Mega Rats' middle infield.
807712.recentAnalysis: Keaschall is showing his raw youth through 22 games with a .212 AVG and .543 OPS for Minnesota — the Mega Rats are being patient while he adjusts to big-league pitching.

807713.name: Matt Shaw
807713.background: A promising free agent third baseman/shortstop in CBA, Matt Shaw arrived as the Cubs' most coveted position player prospect with a mature offensive approach, developing power, and the athleticism to handle multiple infield positions. Available for any CBA team to claim, he profiles as a .280-hitting utility infielder with 20-HR upside as he continues to develop. His combination of contact, walks, and positional flexibility make him one of the better developmental free agent options available.
807713.recentAnalysis: Shaw has had a promising early showing: .264/.310/.434 with 2 HR and 9 RBI through 19 games — solid contact and counting stat contributions from a young player finding his footing at the MLB level.

807799.name: Masataka Yoshida
807799.background: A free agent DH/outfielder in CBA with one of the best pure contact profiles available, Masataka Yoshida brought elite bat-to-ball skills from Japan's NPB and has maintained that contact excellence at the MLB level — posting batting averages above .300 with excellent walk rates and modest power in Boston's lineup. Available for any CBA team looking to add a high-average bat, he profiles as a .300-hitting DH/OF with 15-HR upside and consistent on-base production. His contact skills are among the best of any available free agent.
807799.recentAnalysis: Yoshida has had a nice early stretch in limited action: .310/.474/.414 (0.888 OPS) with 0 HR, 5 RBI, and 8 walks in 38 PA across 12 games — the elite contact and OBP skills that define his profile are fully on display.

808967.name: Yoshinobu Yamamoto
808967.background: Yoshinobu Yamamoto arrived from Japan with the largest pitching contract in history and has backed up every dollar of it, combining a plus sinker with one of the best splitters in baseball to rank among the game's elite starters for the Dodgers. The Manhattan Mega Rats landed him as their staff ace, giving them a frontline starter capable of sub-3.00 ERA production when healthy. Yamamoto's command and strikeout-to-walk ratio are what separate him from the rest of the CBA's pitching corps.
808967.recentAnalysis: Yamamoto has been sharp early in 2026 with a 2.10 ERA, 21 K, and 0.82 WHIP over 25.2 innings through 4 starts — the Mega Rats' ace is living up to his billing.

808975.name: Hyeseong Kim
808975.background: A free agent middle infielder in CBA with elite speed and solid contact skills, Hyeseong Kim arrived from the KBO as a slick-fielding shortstop with exceptional stolen base ability and a disciplined plate approach. Available for any CBA team looking to bolster their speed categories, he profiles as a .280-hitting infielder with 25-plus stolen base upside and the defensive ability to play every day at shortstop or second base.
808975.recentAnalysis: Kim has made a promising early showing: .292/.400/.500 (0.900 OPS) with 1 HR, 3 RBI, and 3 SB across 11 games in 30 PA — excellent production in a small sample with the speed already contributing meaningfully.

808982.name: Jung Hoo Lee
808982.background: Jung Hoo Lee is a contact-first outfielder for the Giants who came over from the KBO with an excellent reputation for making contact and producing high batting averages, delivering a quality debut season in San Francisco after arriving as a highly touted free agent. The Portland Chinook added him as a lineup complement to their already strong offensive core, counting on his contact ability and athletic defense to round out their roster. Lee's batting average profile gives the Chinook a floor-raising presence at a corner spot.
808982.recentAnalysis: Lee has a .244 AVG and .658 OPS through 22 games — modest numbers that the Chinook are hoping will improve as he gets more comfortable in his second MLB season.

668904.name: Royce Lewis
668904.background: One of the most explosive young shortstops in baseball, Royce Lewis is a power-speed threat whose career has been repeatedly derailed by torn ACLs — he missed most of 2022 and all of 2023. When healthy he has shown legitimate top-10 fantasy upside, hitting .300+ with 20+ HR pace in limited looks, and he's shifted to third base for Minnesota. His floor is essentially zero given the injury history, but his ceiling when on the field is elite.
668904.recentAnalysis: Lewis is off to a rocky start in 2026, hitting .196/.305/.396 with 3 HR and 2 SB through 65 PA — the power is there but the batting average and on-base percentage are suppressed. He's historically slow out of the gates and a strong second half should be expected if he stays healthy.

669257.name: Will Smith
669257.background: The best-hitting catcher in the National League for a multi-year stretch, Will Smith is a switch-hitting backstop for the Dodgers whose proximity to elite talent makes him one of the most run-friendly catchers in baseball. He brings legitimate power (20+ HR potential), solid on-base skills, and the RBI volume that comes with batting behind Ohtani and Freeman. A reliable top-5 catcher in standard formats.
669257.recentAnalysis: Smith is producing steadily with .266/.360/.369 and 3 HR through 89 PA — his OPS is a tick below normal but the contact and walks are solid, and the power upside in Dodger Stadium should spike as the season progresses.

605483.name: Blake Snell
605483.background: A two-time Cy Young Award winner (2018 AL, 2023 NL) with some of the nastiest raw stuff in the sport, Blake Snell is a high-strikeout lefty who signed with the Dodgers after his career year with San Diego. His elite K rate (12+ per 9) is countered by high walk totals and a history of abbreviated outings, but when locked in he is a fantasy ace. Durability and control remain the perennial concerns.
605483.recentAnalysis: Snell has yet to take the mound in 2026 — he has not appeared in a game this season and his status is uncertain. Until he pitches, use with extreme caution in any format.

695238.name: Will Wagner
695238.background: A versatile infielder who emerged as a solid contributor for Houston before being traded, Will Wagner offers multi-position eligibility at 2B/SS/3B and a well-rounded offensive profile — decent contact rates, some pop, and enough athleticism to play everywhere in the infield. He's a depth piece rather than a star, but his flexibility makes him useful on thin rosters.
695238.recentAnalysis: Wagner has no 2026 stats on record yet, likely starting the season on the injured list or in a bench role without regular at-bats. Monitor his status before investing.

687515.name: Colby Thomas
687515.background: A speedy outfield prospect for the Athletics, Colby Thomas is a defense-first center fielder whose fantasy value is almost entirely tied to stolen base production. He has 30+ SB potential at full playing time but limited pop and a low enough on-base percentage that his counting stats depend heavily on hitting near the top of the lineup.
687515.recentAnalysis: Thomas has just 4 PA so far in 2026 and has not established a regular role — with the A's in rebuild mode his playing time will be sporadic. Worth stashing for the speed if he breaks into the lineup consistently.

543257.name: Randal Grichuk
543257.background: A veteran right-handed power hitter, Randal Grichuk has been a useful fantasy piece as a fourth outfielder who can produce 20 HR when given extended playing time. He's a free swinger with below-average contact and walk rates but above-average raw power, making him a boom-or-bust streaming option when someone goes down in Arizona's outfield.
543257.recentAnalysis: Grichuk is struggling with a .194/.212/.323 line through 33 PA — no home runs and minimal walks suggest he's in a slump and not currently a productive roster asset.

668731.name: Jake Cronenworth
668731.background: A switch-hitting infielder who was one of the more pleasant fantasy surprises of the early 2020s, Jake Cronenworth offers multi-position eligibility at 1B/2B/SS with a line-drive approach and solid contact rates. He's never been a power hitter but his batting average, walks, and eligibility have made him a useful middle-infield piece. His production has regressed from his 2021 peak.
668731.recentAnalysis: Cronenworth is off to a rough start with .155/.303/.242 and 1 HR in 99 PA — the contact and power are both well below his norms and he's currently not a viable fantasy starter in most formats.

688665.name: Brady Basso
688665.background: A soft-tossing lefty for the Athletics, Brady Basso profiles as a command-over-stuff starter who relies on sequencing, movement, and inducing weak contact rather than overpowering hitters. He has limited strikeout upside but can be effective against left-handed heavy lineups. A back-end streaming option in deeper formats.
688665.recentAnalysis: Basso has just 0.2 IP in 2026, making him effectively unavailable at this point in the season — he's not established in the Athletics rotation yet.

676265.name: Connor Norby
676265.background: A second baseman prospect for Miami, Connor Norby has shown above-average contact skills and pop at the minor league level and has been given opportunities to establish himself with the Marlins. He has legitimate power-speed upside for a middle infielder with his combination of exit velocity and athleticism, making him a good deeper-league watch.
676265.recentAnalysis: Norby is off to a decent start with .229/.340/.388 and 3 HR through 97 PA — modest production but the contact rate and walks are fine, and more power should come as he gets comfortable.

670032.name: David Hamilton
670032.background: A speedy shortstop for the Red Sox, David Hamilton's fantasy value is entirely built around his elite stolen base ability — he has 30-plus SB upside when playing daily. His batting average and on-base skills are weak enough to be a liability in other categories, but he's one of the more valuable pure speed options available in the middle of a deep lineup.
670032.recentAnalysis: Hamilton is producing nicely in the speed department with .234 AVG and 5 SB through 80 PA — if he maintains a regular lineup spot the stolen base production will be the main attraction all season.

645302.name: Victor Robles
645302.background: Once a highly-touted top prospect for Washington, Victor Robles never fully developed his bat and was eventually traded to Seattle. He's a plus defender in center field with elite speed, but his contact rates and power have been consistently underwhelming in the majors. His fantasy value is limited to stolen bases in deep formats.
645302.recentAnalysis: Robles has just 13 PA in 2026 and has not established a regular role in Seattle — with limited at-bats available he's not currently a viable fantasy asset.

688787.name: Sean Burke
688787.background: A hard-throwing right-hander for the White Sox, Sean Burke features a mid-to-upper-90s fastball and a developing slider that give him swing-and-miss upside in the rotation. Chicago's rebuild gives him a clear path to regular starts, and his strikeout ceiling makes him worth monitoring in deeper leagues despite pitching for a poor team.
688787.recentAnalysis: Burke has been one of the quiet early surprises of 2026, posting a 3.21 ERA and 1.04 WHIP with 24 Ks across 33.2 IP — the command has been excellent and he's a legitimate streaming option on Chicago's weak squad.

596129.name: Dominic Smith
596129.background: A left-handed hitting first baseman with a pure stroke, Dominic Smith has bounced around several organizations after showing promise early in his career with the Mets. His calling card is quality contact and a good eye at the plate; his liability is limited power and a career marked by inconsistent playing time. He's a useful bench bat when healthy and starting.
596129.recentAnalysis: Smith is having a very strong early 2026, hitting .349/.382/.562 with 4 HR through 68 PA — the batting average and power have both shown up early and he's worth starting wherever he's in the lineup.

676691.name: Andruw Monasterio
676691.background: A versatile utility infielder for Milwaukee, Andruw Monasterio offers multi-position eligibility across the infield and a contact-oriented approach. He's not a high-ceiling player but his lineup flexibility and solid contact rates make him a useful option when a team has multiple injured infielders. A 15-HR, 10-SB type with good batting average floor.
676691.recentAnalysis: Monasterio is holding his own with .244/.357/.369 and 1 HR and 1 SB through 45 PA — acceptable production but nothing that demands a roster spot in non-deep leagues.

669200.name: Davis Schneider
669200.background: A right-handed hitting outfielder who burst onto the scene with a legendary debut series for the Blue Jays in 2023, Davis Schneider has been trying to replicate that breakout performance. He has solid raw power and a good eye at the plate but his strikeout rate and contact are inconsistent enough to create big swings in batting average.
669200.recentAnalysis: Schneider is struggling with a .140/.302/.256 line through 53 PA — the walks are there but the contact and power have not materialized early. A wait-and-see situation until he finds his swing.

666152.name: Josh Jung
666152.background: A first-round pick who was expected to anchor third base for Texas for years, Josh Jung has shown impressive power and a solid two-strike approach when healthy. His 2025 was disrupted by injury but his combination of raw power (.500+ SLG potential) and positional scarcity at 3B make him a strong buy-low target when available.
666152.recentAnalysis: Jung is off to a very strong 2026, hitting .308/.380/.540 with 4 HR through 100 PA — the power is fully live and he looks healthy. A legitimate top-10 third baseman when starting every day.

664034.name: Austin Hays
664034.background: A left-handed hitting outfielder with solid contact rates and some power, Austin Hays is a steady contributor who hit .280+ with 15+ HR in his best Baltimore seasons before moving to Cincinnati. He's not a fantasy star but offers useful counting stats with minimal batting average damage — a reliable fourth outfielder in standard formats.
664034.recentAnalysis: Hays is off to a slow start with .219/.303/.283 and just 1 HR through 33 PA — well below his typical offensive output. He tends to heat up as the season progresses so patience is warranted.

669720.name: Blake Perkins
669720.background: A speedy center fielder for Milwaukee, Blake Perkins is a leadoff-type with elite athleticism and stolen base ability but a bat that has produced inconsistently at the MLB level. His value is primarily speed-based — he can reach 20-25 SB when playing regularly — though his batting average and power both limit his overall fantasy ceiling.
669720.recentAnalysis: Perkins is struggling to make contact early, hitting .132 with a .461 OPS through 45 PA and just 1 SB — the speed hasn't been activated yet and the batting average is a red flag. Give it time before selling low.

650496.name: Willi Castro
650496.background: A utility infielder who provides multi-position eligibility across the infield and occasional outfield, Willi Castro is a contact-oriented switch-hitter who will never be a power source but can contribute batting average and a handful of steals. His versatility keeps him on big league rosters and makes him useful in deeper leagues.
650496.recentAnalysis: Castro is producing at a decent clip with .246/.329/.335 and 1 HR and 2 SB through 76 PA — solid utility production without a standout category. Worth rostering in deep leagues for eligibility.

686720.name: Thomas Saggese
686720.background: An infield prospect for St. Louis with solid bat-to-ball skills and developing power, Thomas Saggese has shown the contact skills to hit for average with gap power potential. He's been given opportunities with the Cardinals and profiles as a 15-HR, solid-average infielder.
686720.recentAnalysis: Saggese is off to a rough start with .180/.296/.281 and 0 HR through 54 PA — below his expected offensive output and not currently a viable fantasy starter.

697553.name: Carson Palmquist
697553.background: A left-handed pitching prospect for Colorado, Carson Palmquist was a top-100 prospect who features a plus curveball and good feel for pitching. The Coors Field environment makes his fantasy value complicated — away starts are viable streaming options while home starts are typically high-risk.
697553.recentAnalysis: Palmquist has not appeared in 2026 yet, with no stats on record. His debut is expected but the timeline is unclear — watch for his call-up announcement.

605137.name: Josh Bell
605137.background: A switch-hitting first baseman who hit 37 HR for Pittsburgh in 2019, Josh Bell has bounced around multiple organizations without recapturing that peak but remains a useful power-on-base contributor. With Washington he gets regular at-bats and can contribute 20 HR and a solid walk rate in favorable conditions. A middle-tier first base option.
605137.recentAnalysis: Bell is producing steadily with .234/.373/.339 and 3 HR through 109 PA — the walks and on-base skills are strong but the power and average are modest. He's a useful if unexciting first base option.

608577.name: Zack Littell
608577.background: A right-handed starter for Tampa Bay who converted from relief with mixed results, Zack Littell relies on a deceptive delivery and a varied pitch mix rather than overpowering stuff. His strikeout rate is modest and he's prone to stretches of poor command, making him a risky fantasy option even with regular starts.
608577.recentAnalysis: Littell is struggling significantly in 2026 with a 7.56 ERA and 1.68 WHIP across 25 IP — he's been one of the worst pitchers in baseball by ERA and is not currently startable in any format.

687799.name: Jose Tena
687799.background: A young shortstop prospect for Washington, Jose Tena is a contact-first hitter who uses the whole field and has solid speed for stolen base production. His power ceiling is modest but his contact approach and athleticism give him a path to contributing batting average and 10-15 steals if he secures regular at-bats.
687799.recentAnalysis: Tena is off to a decent start with .277/.333/.349 and 1 HR through 50 PA — the contact is there but the power and speed haven't really emerged yet. A monitoring candidate rather than an active add.

658668.name: Andrew Vaughn
658668.background: The third overall pick in 2019, Andrew Vaughn has been a steady if unspectacular contributor for the White Sox who offers batting average and some first base power without being a top-tier producer. His plus contact skills give him a higher batting average floor than most power hitters, and he's a reasonable streaming option when Chicago's lineup is healthy.
658668.recentAnalysis: Vaughn has just 4 PA in 2026 — he's currently not in the everyday lineup and is not a viable fantasy option at this time.

661403.name: Jose Caballero
661403.background: A speedy utility player for Tampa Bay who provides multi-position eligibility and elite stolen base ability, Jose Caballero is one of the better pure speed sources in the league when playing full-time. His batting average and power are modest, but his on-base skills are good enough to get on base for those steals. A go-to stolen base specialist in deep formats.
661403.recentAnalysis: Caballero is absolutely flying to start 2026 with .271/.379/.360 and 3 HR but an outstanding 11 SB through 102 PA — the stolen bases are elite and he's delivering genuine multi-category value.

572122.name: J.P. Crawford
572122.background: A patient left-handed hitting shortstop for Seattle, J.P. Crawford has built his value around elite walk rates and solid defensive play rather than power or speed. His OBP has consistently been 40+ points above his batting average, making him more valuable in OBP leagues. He provides batting average stability without home run or stolen base production.
572122.recentAnalysis: Crawford is hitting .186/.284/.332 through 87 PA — a slow start with limited power, though his walk rate suggests the batting average will recover. Not currently worth starting in standard leagues.

676534.name: Yoan Moncada
676534.background: A former top prospect who delivered flashes of excellence for the White Sox — including a strong 2019 season — Yoan Moncada has been derailed by injuries in recent years and signed with the Angels attempting a comeback. When healthy he has shown 20 HR and 10 SB ability from the third base spot but durability is the central question.
676534.recentAnalysis: Moncada is off to a moderate start with .189/.308/.371 and 3 HR through 91 PA — the power is showing up but the average and contact are below his peak levels. The comeback is in progress but he's not yet the player of 2019.

683623.name: Brooks Lee
683623.background: A first-round shortstop prospect for Minnesota, Brooks Lee was one of the more polished college bats in recent drafts — a switch-hitter with a mature approach, gap power, and solid defense. He was given a real opportunity to claim the Minnesota SS job and has the contact skills to hit near the top of a good lineup.
683623.recentAnalysis: Lee is producing well in his first extended MLB look with .253/.349/.415 and 5 HR and 1 SB through 95 PA — the approach is advanced for his age and he's a legitimate fantasy SS option going forward.

672515.name: Casey Schmitt
672515.background: A third baseman for the Giants who profiles as a defensive standout with developing offensive tools, Casey Schmitt is a right-handed hitter with gap power and a disciplined approach. He's not a prototypical fantasy third baseman in terms of home runs, but his contact and on-base skills give him batting average floor and a path to useful counting stats in a competitive San Francisco lineup.
672515.recentAnalysis: Schmitt is having an excellent start to 2026 with .296/.396/.495 and 4 HR through 89 PA — both the power and average are exceeding expectations and he's currently worth starting as a third baseman.

622534.name: Alek Thomas
622534.background: A speedy center fielder for Arizona who plays above-average defense and offers a useful combination of batting average and steals, Alek Thomas is the kind of player who contributes across multiple categories without dominating any of them. He has legitimate 15-HR, 20-SB upside from the top of a strong Diamondbacks lineup and can be a solid multi-category contributor.
622534.recentAnalysis: Thomas is off to a decent start with .213/.313/.340 and 2 HR and 4 SB through 80 PA — the speed is showing early and even if the average stays around .213 the stolen bases and run-scoring potential keep him viable.

650093.name: Luis Rengifo
650093.background: A utility infielder for the Angels with multi-position eligibility, Luis Rengifo is a contact hitter who can play second base, shortstop, or third base. His fantasy appeal is mostly eligibility-based — he contributes a few categories across the board without excelling in any — but in a year with a full-time role he can be a useful piece on thin rosters.
650093.recentAnalysis: Rengifo is struggling with a .173/.224/.279 line through 85 PA — well below his expected output, this is a significant slump that makes him not worth starting until he turns it around.

628406.name: Ha-seong Kim
628406.background: A slick-fielding shortstop from Korea, Ha-seong Kim established himself as a multi-position defender with surprising power and above-average contact for San Diego. He suffered a shoulder injury that cost him significant time and was one of the notable 2025 IL casualties. When healthy he's a solid middle-infield option with 15+ HR and 15+ SB potential.
628406.recentAnalysis: Ha-seong Kim has not yet appeared in 2026 — he is likely working back from the shoulder injury and his return timeline is unclear. Do not roster until he's confirmed healthy and in the lineup.

665625.name: Jake Meyers
665625.background: A center fielder for Houston who profiles as a solid fourth outfielder with some gap power and decent contact rates, Jake Meyers has shown he can hold his own when inserted into the Astros lineup. He's not a regular starter and his role can shift significantly with Houston's roster moves, limiting his fantasy upside to streaming situations.
665625.recentAnalysis: Meyers is producing in a limited role with .243/.326/.378 and 1 HR and 1 SB through 43 PA — moderate production that makes him a streaming option only when starting consistently.

694194.name: Julian Aguiar
694194.background: A right-handed pitching prospect for Cincinnati, Julian Aguiar is working his way into the Reds rotation with a solid arsenal and developing command. He lacks the high-end strikeout stuff of a top prospect but his pitch mix and feel for pitching give him a chance to be a backend rotation contributor.
694194.recentAnalysis: Aguiar has no 2026 stats on record yet — he has not debuted or is on the injured list. Not a current fantasy asset.

669004.name: Gabriel Moreno
669004.background: One of the top catching prospects of recent years, Gabriel Moreno was acquired by Arizona in the Daulton Varsho trade and has shown exceptional contact skills and a patient approach at the plate for a catcher. He profiles as a .280+ hitter with double-digit home run upside — one of the better options at a thin position in fantasy.
669004.recentAnalysis: Moreno is showing his trademark contact skills with .275/.400/.333 through 45 PA — no home runs yet but the on-base percentage and batting average are excellent for a catcher and the power should eventually arrive.

694568.name: Tim Tawa
694568.background: A young outfielder in the Arizona system, Tim Tawa is working to establish himself at the major league level with a contact-first approach and some athleticism. He doesn't project as a power source but has speed and can contribute across multiple categories in a bench role for a competitive lineup.
694568.recentAnalysis: Tawa is off to a rough start with .146/.208/.296 and 1 HR through 48 PA — not enough production to justify a roster spot in any format at this stage.

686850.name: Denzel Clarke
686850.background: An outfield prospect for the Athletics with exceptional athleticism and raw tools, Denzel Clarke was a two-sport athlete (baseball and football) with legitimate 5-tool potential. His speed is well above average and he has developing power, though his contact and pitch recognition need refinement at the MLB level.
686850.recentAnalysis: Clarke is struggling to hit at the MLB level, batting .170/.267/.150 with 2 SB through 60 PA — the speed is showing but the contact rate is too low to generate useful counting stats currently.

669177.name: Eury Perez
669177.background: One of the more exciting young pitching prospects of recent years, Eury Perez is a 6-foot-8 right-hander for Miami whose combination of elite height, extension, and a nasty changeup generates significant strikeout rates. He debuted as a teenager and showed flashes of ace-caliber stuff before injury issues — a high-ceiling arm who is worth monitoring closely.
669177.recentAnalysis: Perez is back and showing real promise with a 4.60 ERA and 1.40 WHIP across 31.1 IP and an impressive 33 Ks — the strikeout rate is elite and while the ERA needs improvement, the trajectory is encouraging for a return to his 2023 form.

669270.name: Grayson Rodriguez
669270.background: A former top pitching prospect for Baltimore whose career has been disrupted by elbow issues, Grayson Rodriguez has a plus fastball-changeup combination that generates weak contact and strikeouts when healthy. He's shown top-15 SP potential in his healthy appearances and Baltimore gives him a strong defensive team behind him.
669270.recentAnalysis: Rodriguez has not appeared in 2026 yet — he is likely working back from his elbow injury and the timeline for his return is uncertain. Do not roster until he's confirmed healthy and in the rotation.

694940.name: Michael Helman
694940.background: A young shortstop prospect for Texas, Michael Helman is a defense-first middle infielder who is still developing his offensive game at the professional level. He profiles more as a utility depth piece than a fantasy starter at this stage of his career.
694940.recentAnalysis: Helman has no 2026 stats on record — he is not currently on the active roster or is in a non-playing role. Not a fantasy asset at this time.

671679.name: Joey Wiemer
671679.background: A power-arm outfielder for Milwaukee, Joey Wiemer was a fourth-round pick who rocketed through the minors on the strength of his raw power and above-average arm. He's a high-strikeout, high-power profile — boom or bust at the plate — and his athleticism makes him a legitimate 25-30 HR threat when his contact improves.
671679.recentAnalysis: Wiemer is off to a genuinely impressive start with .320/.395/.599 and 3 HR through 58 PA — the power is live and the early batting average is well above expectations. Currently worth starting in all formats.

680695.name: Yilber Diaz
680695.background: A right-handed pitching prospect for Arizona, Yilber Diaz is working his way into professional baseball with a developing arsenal. He's not yet established at the major league level and his fantasy relevance is limited until he earns a consistent rotation spot.
680695.recentAnalysis: Diaz has no 2026 stats on record and is not a current fantasy asset.

656945.name: Aaron Civale
656945.background: A right-handed starter with solid control and a varied pitch mix, Aaron Civale carved out a consistent rotation role with Cleveland before moving through trades. He's not a strikeout artist but his above-average command, ground-ball tendencies, and ability to pitch deep into games make him a viable backend streaming option in matchup-friendly situations.
656945.recentAnalysis: Civale is pitching effectively to start 2026 with a 3.86 ERA and 1.32 WHIP across 25.2 IP with 21 Ks — solid ratios and a viable streaming option when facing a weak lineup.

674949.name: Victor Scott II
674949.background: A blazing-fast outfield prospect for St. Louis, Victor Scott II was one of the most anticipated speedster call-ups with elite stolen base upside — he stole 94 bases across two minor league seasons. His bat is extremely light and his on-base percentage is a concern, but 40+ SB potential makes him one of the most exciting fantasy speed assets in baseball.
674949.recentAnalysis: Scott is struggling to get on base in 2026, hitting .191/.285/.163 with just 3 SB through 79 PA — the speed is there but the batting average and power are both concerning. He needs to get on base more consistently to fully unlock his value.

688898.name: Drew Gilbert
688898.background: A first-round outfield prospect for San Francisco, Drew Gilbert is a college-polished bat with a contact-oriented approach and legitimate gap power. He was one of the more advanced college hitters in his draft class and his solid pitch recognition should translate well to the majors.
688898.recentAnalysis: Gilbert is off to a fine start with .265/.378/.382 and 2 HR through 37 PA — the walks and contact are showing up and he looks like a contributor in San Francisco's lineup.

592743.name: Joc Pederson
592743.background: A left-handed hitting outfielder with a long track record of mashing right-handed pitching, Joc Pederson is a platoon threat who has produced 20+ HR in multiple seasons with some of the best home run rates in the league against righties. His defensive value is minimal but his power against right-handers makes him valuable in the right hitting environment.
592743.recentAnalysis: Pederson is off to a slow start with .230/.347/.325 and just 1 HR through 75 PA — the power has been absent early and he's currently not living up to his reputation. A regression to the mean toward more home runs is expected.

694524.name: Richard Fitts
694524.background: A right-handed pitching prospect for Boston, Richard Fitts features a solid four-pitch mix and the durability to eat innings as a backend starter. He doesn't have an overpowering profile but his command and feel for pitching give him a path to contributing for a contending Red Sox team.
694524.recentAnalysis: Fitts has no 2026 stats on record — he has not yet pitched this season and is likely starting the year in a minor league or injured role.

672343.name: Ildemaro Vargas
672343.background: A journeyman utility infielder, Ildemaro Vargas has bounced through multiple organizations providing positional flexibility and occasional pop off the bench. He's not a regular starter but his multi-position eligibility keeps him on 40-man rosters and he can deliver useful counting stats in a short period when playing regularly.
672343.recentAnalysis: Vargas has been one of the most surprising early contributors of 2026, batting .367/1.105 OPS with 6 HR through 82 PA — an extraordinary hot streak that is unlikely to be fully sustainable but worth riding while it lasts.

685506.name: Dane Myers
685506.background: A young outfield prospect for Miami, Dane Myers is an athletic player with speed and developing tools who is fighting for regular playing time in a rebuilding Marlins organization. He has the athleticism to contribute multiple categories but needs consistent at-bats to develop.
685506.recentAnalysis: Myers is off to a decent start with .263/.362/.383 and 1 HR and 1 SB through 47 PA — showing he can handle MLB pitching, though his role needs to solidify before he's a standard-league asset.

592206.name: Nick Castellanos
592206.background: A right-handed hitting outfielder who signed a massive deal with Philadelphia, Nick Castellanos has been one of the most consistent run producers in baseball over a multi-year span. His best work came in Cincinnati and Detroit, where he hit .300+ with 20+ HR. He has declined from his peak but still offers solid counting stat production from a deep Phillies lineup.
592206.recentAnalysis: Castellanos is off to a very rough start with .146/.176/.229 and 0 HR through 51 PA — a significant slump that has made him a lineup liability so far. His track record suggests a correction is coming but the early 2026 has been alarming.

680760.name: Nick Sogard
680760.background: A versatile utility infielder for Boston, Nick Sogard provides multi-position eligibility and solid contact skills but limited power. He's the kind of player who finds a roster spot due to defensive versatility rather than offensive production.
680760.recentAnalysis: Sogard has no 2026 stats on record — he has not accumulated any at-bats this season and is not a current fantasy asset.

700489.name: Cody Freeman
700489.background: A young infield prospect for Texas, Cody Freeman is working his way through the system as a versatile middle infield option. He has developing tools but is not yet established at the major league level.
700489.recentAnalysis: Freeman has no 2026 stats — not currently on the active major league roster.

669061.name: Gavin Lux
669061.background: A former top prospect for the Dodgers, Gavin Lux is a left-handed hitting second baseman with a solid approach and gap power who has dealt with a torn ACL and inconsistent playing time. Now with Cincinnati, he has a chance at regular at-bats. He's a 15-HR, 10-SB type who contributes batting average and rarely hurts ratios.
669061.recentAnalysis: Lux has no 2026 stats on record — he has not yet appeared in a game this season and his availability is uncertain.

656548.name: Jack Flaherty
656548.background: A right-handed ace candidate for Detroit, Jack Flaherty had a dominant run with St. Louis before injuries and inconsistency led to a trade to Detroit. He features a dynamic fastball-slider combination with elite swing-and-miss potential when on. His upside is a 15-win, 200-K season but the injury history and inconsistency are real concerns.
656548.recentAnalysis: Flaherty is off to a rough start with a 5.33 ERA and 1.74 WHIP across 25.1 IP with 28 Ks — the strikeout rate is solid but home runs and walks are driving up the ERA. He needs to clean up the peripherals to be startable.

624428.name: Isiah Kiner-Falefa
624428.background: A utility infielder known for elite contact rates and stolen base ability rather than power, Isiah Kiner-Falefa is a speed-and-average profile player who contributes SB in volume at the top of a good lineup. His lack of power limits his overall fantasy ceiling but he's a useful tool in categories leagues with stolen base needs.
624428.recentAnalysis: Kiner-Falefa has .212/.343/.227 with 2 SB through 35 PA — the speed is there in early going but the batting average and total lack of power make him a stolen-base specialist only.

694189.name: Marcelo Mayer
694189.background: A first-round shortstop prospect for Boston who was the fourth overall pick in the 2022 draft, Marcelo Mayer has a polished left-handed stroke, excellent baseball instincts, and legitimate 15-20 HR potential with high contact rates. He has all the makings of a franchise shortstop and should be one of the better fantasy SSs once he settles in.
694189.recentAnalysis: Mayer is showing his typical disciplined approach with .224/.360/.291 and 1 HR and 2 SB through 76 PA — the walks are impressive for a rookie but the power and average need to develop further to make him a standard-league starter.

666967.name: Andy Ibanez
666967.background: A utility infielder for Detroit, Andy Ibanez provides defensive versatility and modest offensive contributions as a role player. He lacks the upside to be a fantasy starter in standard formats but offers multi-position eligibility in deeper leagues.
666967.recentAnalysis: Ibanez is off to a very slow start with .118 AVG and .285 OPS through 18 PA — not a viable fantasy asset at this time.

685544.name: Graham Pauley
685544.background: A contact-hitting first baseman / corner infielder for Miami, Graham Pauley uses the whole field with gap power and a solid plate approach. He's not a traditional power-first first baseman but has the contact skills to hit for a reasonable average. A depth option in deeper leagues.
685544.recentAnalysis: Pauley is hitting .217/.328/.304 with 1 HR and 1 SB through 64 PA — modest production that doesn't demand a roster spot in standard formats.

665750.name: Leody Taveras
665750.background: A speedy switch-hitting center fielder for Texas, Leody Taveras profiles as an above-average defender with elite speed and developing offensive skills. His value is primarily stolen base-driven — he has 20-25 SB upside — but his batting average and power have been inconsistent in the majors.
665750.recentAnalysis: Taveras is off to a nice start with .288/.394/.403 and 2 HR and 1 SB through 71 PA — the on-base skills are excellent and the power is a pleasant surprise. A viable daily starter in Texas's lineup.

694979.name: Andrew Alvarez
694979.background: A left-handed pitching prospect for Washington, Andrew Alvarez is a finesse-style arm who relies on command and pitch movement rather than velocity. He has a chance to contribute as a backend rotation piece for a rebuilding Nationals team.
694979.recentAnalysis: Alvarez has just 4.1 IP in 2026 with a 0.00 ERA and 0.92 WHIP — a very small sample suggesting a recent call-up or limited usage so far. Not enough data to assess.

694652.name: Carson Whisenhunt
694652.background: A lefty pitching prospect for San Francisco with an advanced feel for pitching, Carson Whisenhunt brings a plus changeup and good command to the mound. He lacks overwhelming velocity but his pitch-to-contact approach and deception make him a viable backend starter.
694652.recentAnalysis: Whisenhunt has no 2026 stats on record — he has not yet appeared in a major league game this season.

669455.name: Triston Casas
669455.background: A massive left-handed first baseman for Boston who was one of the top power prospects in baseball, Triston Casas combines elite power (30+ HR upside) with an exceptional walk rate that gives him tremendous on-base skills. His 2025 was disrupted by a knee injury that ended his season early — when healthy he profiles as a top-5 first baseman in fantasy.
669455.recentAnalysis: Casas has no 2026 stats on record — he is likely still recovering from his knee injury and his return timeline is uncertain. A high-priority monitor for when he comes back.

664031.name: Harrison Bader
664031.background: A right-handed center fielder known primarily for his elite defense, Harrison Bader has shown occasional bursts of offensive production with some power and speed. His value is almost entirely based on playing time and whether he's hot in a given stretch — not a reliable fantasy asset but occasionally useful in deep formats.
664031.recentAnalysis: Bader is off to a very poor start with .115/.191/.192 and 1 HR through 55 PA — not producing at a level that merits a roster spot in any format currently.

665923.name: Tyrone Taylor
665923.background: A right-handed outfielder who has bounced around several organizations, Tyrone Taylor is a streaky power hitter who can put up excellent numbers in hot streaks but offers no consistency as a full-season producer. He's primarily a streaming option when in a platoon or during an opponent's lefty-heavy stretch.
665923.recentAnalysis: Taylor is hitting .214/.289/.325 with 2 HR through 45 PA — moderate production that makes him a streaming option at best when getting regular at-bats.

695395.name: Shane Smith
695395.background: A young right-handed starter for the White Sox, Shane Smith is working to establish himself in a major league rotation as part of Chicago's lengthy rebuild. He has developing stuff and command but is not yet a reliable fantasy option given the team context and his own inconsistency.
695395.recentAnalysis: Smith has struggled badly in his early 2026 appearances, posting a 10.80 ERA and 2.52 WHIP across 8.1 IP — not startable and likely heading back to the minors if the results don't improve.

698454.name: Zach Dezenzo
698454.background: A young infield prospect for Houston, Zach Dezenzo is developing his game in the system and has yet to establish himself at the major league level. Not currently a fantasy asset.
698454.recentAnalysis: Dezenzo has no 2026 stats on record and is not on the active major league roster.

669152.name: Ben Williamson
669152.background: A shortstop for Seattle who has shown solid contact skills and athletic versatility in his early major league exposure, Ben Williamson is a developing middle infield option for the Mariners. He brings a solid approach at the plate and decent speed without a standout offensive category.
669152.recentAnalysis: Williamson is contributing steadily with .260/.341/.329 and 2 SB through 82 PA — a reasonable contact rate and some speed, though the lack of power caps his upside.

695562.name: Eric Wagaman
695562.background: A first baseman-corner infielder for Miami, Eric Wagaman is a big left-handed bat who has shown good power in the minors and is looking to establish himself in the major leagues. He profiles as a power-over-average type who could be a useful depth piece.
695562.recentAnalysis: Wagaman has no 2026 stats on record — he has not appeared in a major league game this season.

663993.name: Shane Baz
663993.background: A right-handed power pitcher for Tampa Bay who was part of the Blake Snell trade, Shane Baz features elite stuff — a 97+ mph fastball and sharp breaking ball — that generated enormous buzz before significant elbow trouble derailed his career. He has top-10 SP upside when healthy and the Rays are carefully managing his return.
663993.recentAnalysis: Baz has made progress in 2026 with a 5.08 ERA and 1.55 WHIP across 28.1 IP and 23 Ks — the strikeout rate is encouraging but the command and home runs allowed need work. His ceiling remains high if the control improves.

694941.name: Caden Dana
694941.background: A young right-handed pitching prospect for the Angels with an intriguing arsenal, Caden Dana is working toward becoming a regular rotation contributor. He has a developing slider that generates swing-and-miss and solid command for his age, making him a longer-term watch rather than an immediate fantasy asset.
694941.recentAnalysis: Dana has no 2026 stats on record — he has not appeared in a major league game this season.

694569.name: Mitch Farris
694569.background: A young left-handed reliever for the Angels, Mitch Farris is working his way into a high-leverage role with a developing arsenal. He has solid strikeout potential but is not yet established enough for fantasy relevance in most formats.
694569.recentAnalysis: Farris has just 4.1 IP in 2026 with a 2.08 ERA — a tiny sample from a reliever not currently accumulating enough innings or saves to be a meaningful fantasy asset.

694957.name: Hunter Dobbins
694957.background: A right-handed pitching prospect for Boston, Hunter Dobbins features a solid four-pitch mix and the ability to pitch deep into games. He's not an ace-profile arm but his command and competitiveness give him a chance to contribute as a backend starter for a competitive Red Sox team.
694957.recentAnalysis: Dobbins has no 2026 stats on record — he has not yet made his MLB debut this season.

666305.name: Miguel Amaya
666305.background: A catching prospect for the Cubs who was a key part of their rebuild, Miguel Amaya is a switch-hitter with solid power and an advanced approach behind the plate. He profiles as a 15-HR catcher with good on-base skills, which at a position this thin makes him a legitimate mid-tier fantasy catcher.
666305.recentAnalysis: Amaya is off to a solid start with .234/.364/.383 and 2 HR through 55 PA — the walks are excellent for a catcher and the power is beginning to emerge. A viable streaming catcher with upside.

642786.name: Ramon Urias
642786.background: A versatile utility infielder for Baltimore who provides multi-position eligibility and solid contact skills, Ramon Urias is more valuable for his defensive flexibility than his offensive production. He can contribute a reasonable batting average in limited at-bats but lacks the power or speed to be a fantasy starter in standard formats.
642786.recentAnalysis: Urias is hitting .180/.322/.376 with 2 HR through 59 PA — the walks and power are present but the batting average drag makes him hard to start in anything but the deepest formats.

663637.name: Nick Loftin
663637.background: A versatile infielder for Kansas City who can play anywhere in the middle infield, Nick Loftin is a contact-first hitter with solid athleticism and developing offensive skills. He's not a power source but his versatility and contact ability give him a path to regular at-bats in the Royals lineup.
663637.recentAnalysis: Loftin is off to a nice start with .292/.345/.458 through 29 PA — hitting well above his expected level early on, though the small sample calls for caution.

694637.name: Brady House
694637.background: A first-round shortstop / third base prospect for Washington, Brady House has impressive raw power for a middle infielder — legitimate 20-25 HR upside — along with solid athleticism. He's been given a real opportunity to contribute for a rebuilding Nationals team and has the ceiling of a 15-HR, 10-SB shortstop.
694637.recentAnalysis: House is off to a solid start with .235/.327/.427 and 3 HR and 1 SB through 110 PA — the power is showing up and he looks like he can handle MLB pitching. A legitimate middle infield option in deeper leagues.

657452.name: Lane Thomas
657452.background: An outfielder who has shown flashes of multi-category production, Lane Thomas is a right-handed hitter with solid speed and some pop who has been most productive when getting regular center field at-bats. He had a breakout 2023 with Washington and offers 15-HR, 15-SB upside when starting every day.
657452.recentAnalysis: Thomas is off to a slow start with .216/.275/.306 and 0 HR but 2 SB through 62 PA — the power is absent early and the average is below his normal levels. Worth monitoring to see if the bat comes around.
